claim: The Voynich text uses a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin where 2-3 Voynich glyphs map to each Latin letter, accounting for the observed entropy gap (3.8627 bits actual vs ~4.0 bits expected for Latin) and the anomalously high hapax ratio (70.1%) caused by variant glyph combinations encoding the same Latin word.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is close to but below Latin ~4.0 bits, consistent with homophones compressing entropy
High hapax ratio 70.1% explainable if multiple glyph-sequences encode same underlying word
25 unique glyphs is large enough to support homophonic mapping onto ~22-letter medieval Latin alphabet
Word-initial and word-final glyph constraints (o,c,q,s,d and y,n,l,r,o) could reflect Latin morphological patterns filtered through a homophonic key
evidence_against: Word entropy 10.4508 bits is very high for a simple homophonic cipher on Latin
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than typical natural language (~1.0), suggesting additional structural transformation
If hapaxes were cipher artifacts, we would expect more near-duplicate word forms differing by one glyph â€” not confirmed
test: Construct a candidate homophonic mapping (2-3 Voynich glyphs per Latin letter) by frequency-matching: rank Voynich glyphs by frequency, rank medieval Latin letters by frequency, assign top-N glyphs to most frequent letters. Decode a 500-word sample and measure bigram frequency correlation between decoded output and a Latin reference corpus.
test_metric: Pearson correlation between decoded bigram frequencies and Latin bigram frequencies; also measure type-token ratio of decoded text vs. raw Latin
test_threshold: Pearson r > 0.7 on bigram frequencies AND decoded hapax ratio drops below 30% would confirm; r < 0.3 or hapax ratio remains above 60% would deny

tests_run.details: The hypothesis is undermined by insufficient glyphs (25) for a 2-3:1 homophonic mapping to 26 Latin letters, lower entropy than expected from redundant encoding, and a hapax ratio inconsistent with repeated plaintext words encoded variantly.
A 2-3 glyph per letter homophonic scheme requires 52-78 glyphs for Latin, but only 25 exist; entropy is too low for such ciphers despite Zipfian flattening being consistent with encipherment.

High hapax ratio (70.1%) is strongly consistent with glyph variants encoding identical Latin words; Zipf exponent (0.8946) is plausible for homophonic redundancy; entropy (3.8627 bits) matches expected compression from variant substitution, though the full hypothesis remains inconclusive pending bigram correlation testing against Latin corpus.

Homophonic ciphers typically increase entropy by expanding the symbol set; the observed lower entropy (3.8627 vs ~4.0) contradicts this mechanism, and the 70.1% hapax ratio is implausibly high for medieval Latin even with variant encodings.

High hapax ratio (70.1%) and modest entropy gap support homophonic encoding, but only 25 glyphs cannot support consistent 2-3 mappings for all 20+ Latin letters, making the scheme incomplete or fundamentally different; Zipf exponent unchanged (0.8946), which is neutral; actual decoding test not yet performed.

A homophonic substitution system with 2-3 glyphs mapping to each of 26 Latin letters requires 52-78 glyphs minimum; the manuscript contains only 25 glyphs, making the proposed system mathematically impossible.

A homophonic cipher mapping 2-3 glyphs per Latin letter cannot be encoded with only 25 total glyphs (requires 40-75); homophonic encoding increases entropy by flattening frequency distributions, contradicting the observed entropy decrease (3.86 vs 4.0 bits); the Zipf exponent of 0.89 with strong fit reflects language structure, not cipher artifacts.

The hypothesis is mathematically incompatible with the observed glyph inventory: 25 glyphs cannot accommodate a 2-3 glyph per letter mapping for medieval Latin (~20-26 letters), which would require 40-78 glyphs; the low entropy and high hapax ratio are insufficient evidence to overcome this fundamental constraint.

The hypothesis cannot accommodate 26 Latin letters with 2-3 Voynich glyphs per letter using only 25 glyphs (would require 52-78 positions); while it plausibly explains the high hapax ratio and preserves Zipf structure, the entropy gap contradicts expectationsâ€”homophonic ciphers increase entropy due to glyph redundancy, not decrease it to 3.8627 bits.

The anomalously high hapax ratio (70.1%) is well-explained by homophonic encoding creating variant spellings of the same underlying Latin word; glyph count and entropy gap are also consistent with the hypothesis.

Entropy reduction, elevated hapax ratio, and flattened Zipf exponent are all consistent with homophonic substitution where each Latin letter maps to 2-3 glyph variants, distributing letter frequencies across multiple symbols and reducing observable glyph entropy.

The observed hapax ratio (70.1%) and entropy gap (3.8627 vs ~4.0 bits) are consistent with homophonic substitution producing variant encodings of the same Latin words, but the hypothesis requires bigram frequency correlation testing on decoded output for definitive validation.

High hapax ratio (70.1%), reduced entropy, and strong Zipf fit (RÂ²=0.9084) support homophonic encoding where variant glyph combinations encode the same Latin word, though the tight glyph count limits full mapping flexibility.

Hypothesis fails mathematical constraint: 25 glyphs cannot support 2-3 variants per ~20 Latin letters (requires 40+ glyphs). Additionally, homophonic ciphers increase entropy through variant encoding, contradicting the observed 3.8627-bit entropy being lower than expected Latin entropy (~4.0 bits).

reasoning: Homophonic substitution is the simplest mechanism that simultaneously explains the entropy gap from Latin, the high hapax ratio, and the glyph positional constraints. Confirming or denying it would strongly narrow the cipher-type search space.
